q: In radioactive decay, the decay rate is proportional to:
exp: Decay law: dN/dt = -λN, so rate depends on remaining nuclei.

q: The SI unit of radioactive activity is:
exp: Activity = 1 decay per second = Becquerel.

q: Half-life of a radioactive substance is the time in which:
exp: Half-life means 50% of initial nuclei decay.

q: The decay constant λ is related to half-life T½ by:
exp: Standard relation: T½ = 0.693 / λ.

q: Which radiation has the highest penetrating power?
exp: Gamma rays are highly penetrating electromagnetic waves.

q: Alpha particles are identical to:
exp: Alpha particles are He²⁺ nuclei (2 protons + 2 neutrons).

q: Beta decay involves emission of:
exp: Beta decay emits electrons (β⁻) or positrons (β⁺).

q: Gamma radiation is:
exp: Gamma rays are high-energy photons.

q: In alpha decay, atomic number decreases by:
exp: Loss of 2 protons reduces atomic number by 2.

q: In beta minus decay, neutron converts into:
exp: n → p + e⁻ + ν̄.

q: Nuclear binding energy is related to:
exp: Binding energy = mass defect × c².

q: Mass defect is:
opts: Difference between nucleon mass and nucleus mass
exp: Mass defect arises due to binding energy.

q: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum for:
exp: Iron nucleus is most stable.

q: Which force holds nucleus together?
exp: Strong nuclear force overcomes repulsion between protons.

q: Fission is:
exp: Fission splits heavy nuclei like U-235.

q: Fusion occurs at:
exp: Fusion requires extremely high temperature (stars).

q: In nuclear reaction, mass-energy is conserved according to:
exp: E = mc² governs nuclear energy changes.

q: Neutron was discovered by:
exp: James Chadwick discovered neutron in 1932.

q: Which particle has no charge?
exp: Neutron is electrically neutral.

q: The unit of nuclear energy is commonly:
exp: Nuclear energies are often measured in electron volts (MeV).

q: Radioactive decay follows:
exp: N = N0 e^{-λt} shows exponential decay.

q: Activity of a radioactive sample decreases with time as:
exp: Activity is proportional to number of undecayed nuclei.

q: Which radiation is stopped by paper?
exp: Alpha particles have very low penetration power.

q: Which radiation is deflected most in electric field?
exp: Beta particles (light electrons) are highly deflected.

q: Half-life depends on:
exp: Half-life is intrinsic to radioactive nucleus.

q: Which is correct?
exp: Beta radiation consists of electrons or positrons.

q: In nuclear fission, energy is released due to:
exp: Mass defect converts into energy.

q: Which is the most stable nucleus?
exp: Medium nuclei like iron have highest stability.

q: Alpha decay equation generally reduces mass number by:
exp: Alpha particle carries 2 protons and 2 neutrons.

q: Which law governs radioactive decay?
exp: Decay follows exponential decrease.

q: One curie is equal to:
exp: Curie is old unit of activity.

q: Which particle is emitted in beta plus decay?
exp: Beta plus decay emits positron.

q: Energy released in nuclear reactions comes from:
exp: E = mc² explains nuclear energy.

q: Which radiation has no charge?
exp: Gamma rays are neutral photons.

q: Decay constant is:
opts: Probability of decay per unit time
exp: λ represents probability of decay per unit time.

q: Which is true about neutron?
exp: Neutron has zero charge but has mass.

q: Fusion reactions power:
exp: Stars produce energy via nuclear fusion.

q: Which process decreases atomic number by 1?
exp: In beta minus decay, neutron becomes proton + electron.

q: Penetration power order is:
exp: Gamma rays penetrate most, alpha least.

q: Which is correct about gamma rays?
exp: Gamma rays are high-energy photons.

q: In nuclear reaction, binding energy per nucleon indicates:
exp: Higher binding energy per nucleon means more stable nucleus.

q: Which is correct about radioactive decay?
exp: Decay is a random nuclear process independent of external conditions.
